title: Irish soda bread
subtitle: No yeast, no kneading, ready in an hour
excerpt: A round soda loaf leavened with bicarbonate of soda and buttermilk, the cross scored before it goes in. Five hundred grams of wholemeal flour, a teaspoon of soda, cold buttermilk, twenty minutes of work, forty minutes in the oven.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the round scored wholemeal loaf leavened with soda documented as a household staple across British and Irish homes. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: soda bread entries using bicarbonate of soda with sour milk as the leavening mechanism. Mrs Rundell, A New System of Domestic Cookery (1806), Project Gutenberg: early soda-leavened quick breads. The Irish soda bread tradition predates reliable commercial yeast; bicarbonate of soda was adopted in Ireland from the 1840s as a practical alternative where ovens were inconsistent.

content.text: Soda bread is the quickest loaf in the house. No yeast, no proving, no kneading, the bicarbonate of soda reacts with the buttermilk the moment the two meet, and the dough goes straight into the oven. From bowl to baked in under an hour.

The dough is wet by bread standards; a 78% hydration with buttermilk rather than water. The wetness is intentional. Wholemeal flour absorbs more liquid than white, and the buttermilk gives the crumb its characteristic slight tang. Handle it briefly, shape it into a round, score it, and bake it. More than a minute of working the dough toughens it.

attrs.body: If you have no buttermilk, pour 400 ml whole milk into a jug and add 1 tablespoon of white wine vinegar or lemon juice. Leave for 10 minutes without stirring. The acid curdles the milk enough to work as the leavening partner.
attrs.title: Buttermilk substitute

content.text: What you need

The recipe uses a blend of wholemeal and plain flour: the wholemeal gives the flavour and the plain keeps the crumb from going brick-dense. A 70/30 blend is the starting point; shift toward all wholemeal if you like a denser loaf, or all plain for a lighter one. The soda is the only leavening, measure it level rather than heaped, or the bread tastes of soap.
Method
Heat the oven and prepare the tray
Heat the oven to 210°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more, an oven thermometer takes the guessing out.
Mix the dry ingredients

Weigh {{wholemeal-flour}} wholemeal flour, {{plain-flour}} plain flour, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} bicarbonate of soda (also called baking soda in American recipes), and {{sea-salt-fine}} fine sea salt into the large bowl. Run your fingers through the bowl to mix the soda evenly. Pockets of undissolved soda cause green streaks in the crumb.
Add the buttermilk

Pour in {{buttermilk}} cold buttermilk and mix with a broad knife or the handle of a wooden spoon. Bring the dough together with ten or twelve strokes. Stop when the flour is incorporated, the dough is rough and shaggy, not smooth, and that is correct. Overworking develops the gluten, which toughens a soda loaf.
Shape and score
Tip the dough onto a lightly floured surface. With floured hands, shape it briefly into a round about 20 cm across and 5 cm deep. Lift it onto the prepared tray. Cut a
deep cross
into the top: two cuts at right angles, each going about two-thirds of the way down through the loaf. The cuts let the centre bake through.
Bake, 40 minutes

Bake for 25 minutes at 210°C fan, then drop the oven to 190°C and bake for a further 15 minutes. The loaf is done when the base sounds hollow when you tap it and an internal probe reads 96°C. The top should be deep brown and the crust firm. Leave the loaf on the tray for 10 minutes before moving to a rack; the crust crisps as it cools.
Troubleshooting

attrs.intro: The four most common problems with soda bread.

items.fix: Weigh everything. Keep mixing to ten or twelve strokes from when the buttermilk goes in. The dough should look shaggy and slightly sticky.
items.cause: Too much flour or too little buttermilk, or the dough was overworked.

items.fix: Measure the soda level, not heaped. Run your fingers through the dry mix before adding the liquid to distribute it.
items.cause: Too much bicarbonate of soda or it wasn't mixed evenly through the dry ingredients.

items.fix: Same as above, mix the soda through the flour thoroughly before the buttermilk goes in.
items.cause: Pockets of undissolved soda reacting unevenly.

items.fix: Keep the round to 20 cm across and no deeper than 5 cm. Drop the temperature after 25 minutes as directed.
items.cause: Loaf too thick, or oven temperature too high at the start (crust set before the centre cooked).

attrs.heading: When things go sideways

content.text: Variations

A white soda bread uses all plain flour and is lighter and softer; bake it at 200°C fan without the temperature drop. A treacle soda bread adds 1 tablespoon of black treacle to the buttermilk before mixing, giving a dark crumb and a slightly sweet, earthy flavour. A seeded version folds 2 tablespoons of mixed seeds (sunflower and pumpkin work well) through the dry ingredients before the buttermilk.
Where this bread sits

Soda bread became practical in Ireland and Britain after the 1840s, when bicarbonate of soda became widely sold in shops. Before that, bread leavening meant yeast or the slow sour of a sourdough culture. The soda loaf was a household staple wherever buttermilk or sour milk was the natural by-product of butter-making. Florence White's 1932 recording of regional British baking includes several variants. The Victorian household manual by Mrs Beeton, from 1861, covers the plain soda method that the recipe here follows.
